title: Somatic Therapy for Survivors of Childhood Emotional Neglect by Neuroscience Culture
excerpt: Discover how somatic therapy heals childhood emotional neglect by addressing body-based trauma and nervous system dysregulation.
body:
Understanding Childhood Emotional Neglect (CEN)

Childhood Emotional Neglect (CEN) is an invisible form of trauma that occurs when a child’s emotional needs are consistently ignored, invalidated, or unmet by caregivers. Unlike overt abuse, CEN is characterized by an absence—lack of attunement, empathy, and emotional availability. This absence leaves deep imprints on the developing nervous system without necessarily leaving visible scars.

Dr. Jonice Webb, a leading expert on CEN, describes it as a “neglect of the emotional connection that children need to grow up feeling safe, valued, and understood.” Survivors often grow into adulthood with a persistent sense of emptiness, difficulty identifying or expressing emotions, and chronic self-doubt. The body remembers what the mind cannot fully articulate.

The Neuroscience of Emotional Neglect

Neuroscientific research reveals how CEN impacts brain development and the nervous system. Bessel van der Kolk, in his seminal work The Body Keeps the Score, emphasizes that trauma is stored in the body and nervous system rather than solely in explicit memory. Emotional neglect during childhood disrupts the development of the brain’s regulatory circuits, especially in areas such as the prefrontal cortex and limbic system, which govern emotional regulation and self-awareness.

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory further explains how early emotional neglect can lead to a chronically dysregulated autonomic nervous system. Survivors may become stuck in states of hypoarousal (emotional numbness, dissociation) or hyperarousal (anxiety, hypervigilance), impairing their ability to feel safe in relationships or even within their own bodies.

Why Traditional Talk Therapy Often Falls Short

Many survivors of CEN have tried traditional talk therapies only to find themselves feeling stuck or misunderstood. This is because talk therapy primarily engages the cognitive, narrative-processing parts of the brain, while the trauma and dysregulation from emotional neglect are encoded somatically—below the level of conscious awareness.

Pat Ogden and her work in Sensorimotor Psychotherapy emphasize the importance of addressing trauma through the body. Without accessing the lived experience of the body and nervous system, talk therapy alone cannot fully resolve the somatic imprints of neglect.

“The body keeps the score; to heal trauma, we must listen to the body’s language.” – Bessel van der Kolk

What is Somatic Therapy?

Somatic therapy is a body-centered approach to healing trauma that integrates the mind and body. It focuses on increasing awareness of bodily sensations, movement, and the autonomic nervous system’s responses as pathways to healing. Somatic therapists guide clients to notice and regulate their physical experiences, helping to restore a sense of safety and connection within the body.

Peter Levine’s approach, Somatic Experiencing, is designed to renegotiate and release traumatic energy stored in the body by tracking sensations and allowing the nervous system to complete incomplete defensive responses. Deb Dana’s work applies Polyvagal Theory to help clients learn to shift between states of nervous system regulation and dysregulation, fostering resilience and social engagement.

How Somatic Therapy Addresses Childhood Emotional Neglect

Survivors of CEN often struggle with a disconnected relationship to their own emotions and bodily sensations. Somatic therapy helps by:

  • Rebuilding interoception: Enhancing awareness of internal bodily signals, which are often dulled or ignored in CEN survivors.
  • Regulating the nervous system: Teaching clients to identify and shift states of hyperarousal or hypoarousal, improving emotional regulation.
  • Releasing trapped energy: Allowing the body to complete defensive responses that were inhibited in childhood.
  • Integrating mind and body: Creating a felt sense of safety and self-compassion that was missing during development.

Somatic therapy also addresses the shame and self-criticism common in CEN by cultivating an embodied sense of presence and acceptance, which Gabor Maté highlights as crucial for trauma recovery.

Practical Somatic Tools for Self-Healing from CEN

While working with a trained somatic therapist is ideal, survivors can begin self-directed healing with these evidence-based tools:

  • Body scans: Regularly tune into the body’s sensations without judgment to reclaim interoceptive awareness (Pat Ogden).
  • Grounding exercises: Use feet on the floor, tactile objects, or mindful movement to anchor nervous system regulation.
  • Tracking sensations: Notice and name physical feelings linked to emotions to build a vocabulary for somatic experience.
  • Breath regulation: Practice slow, diaphragmatic breathing to shift the autonomic nervous system toward safety (Deb Dana).
  • Movement: Gentle stretching, shaking, or yoga can help discharge stored tension and foster embodiment.

Integrating Somatic Therapy with Other Modalities

Somatic therapy pairs well with modalities like Internal Family Systems (IFS), EMDR, and mindfulness. Richard Schwartz’s IFS offers a way to dialogue with internal parts that may have been shut down due to neglect. Francine Shapiro’s EMDR accesses and reprocesses traumatic memories while somatic work ensures the nervous system is regulated during sessions.

Dan Siegel’s concept of mindsight, the capacity to perceive internal experience with curiosity and compassion, is expanded through somatic awareness, supporting survivors in reclaiming agency over their emotional lives.

Finding a Somatic Therapist and What to Expect

When seeking a somatic therapist, look for someone trained in trauma-informed, body-centered modalities such as Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, or Polyvagal-informed therapy. Expect sessions to be slower paced, with a focus on noticing sensation, breath, and subtle nervous system shifts rather than solely talking through stories.

Building safety is the priority. The therapist will support you in developing resources to tolerate and regulate emotions and bodily sensations that emerge. Healing from childhood emotional neglect is a process of re-parenting the nervous system—teaching it how to feel seen, safe, and held.

Conclusion: Reclaiming Wholeness Through Somatic Healing

Childhood Emotional Neglect leaves survivors with invisible wounds that disrupt their relationship to themselves and others. Somatic therapy offers a powerful pathway to healing by addressing the body-based imprint of neglect and restoring nervous system regulation. This embodied approach complements traditional therapies by meeting the trauma where it lives—in the body.

As Bessel van der Kolk reminds us, true recovery is not about forgetting trauma but about learning to live fully with it, in a body that feels safe and alive. For survivors of CEN, somatic therapy can be the key to unlocking that embodied resilience and reclaiming their emotional and physical wellbeing.

References

  • van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Viking.
  • Levine, P. A. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  • Porges, S. W. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-Regulation. W.W. Norton.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W.W. Norton.
  • Webb, J. (2012). Running on Empty: Overcome Your Childhood Emotional Neglect. HarperOne.
  • Maté, G. (2018). In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts: Close Encounters with Addiction. North Atlantic Books.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. W.W. Norton.
  • Schwartz, R. C. (1995). Internal Family Systems Therapy. Guilford Press.
  • Shapiro, F. (2018). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy, Third Edition: Basic Principles, Protocols, and Procedures. Guilford Press.
  • Siegel, D. J. (2010). The Mindful Therapist: A Clinician's Guide to Mindsight and Neural Integration. W.W. Norton.

faqs.q: How does childhood emotional neglect differ from abuse?
faqs.a: Emotional neglect is the absence of emotional support and validation, whereas abuse involves harmful actions. Neglect often goes unnoticed but deeply impacts emotional development.

faqs.q: Can somatic therapy help if traditional therapy didn’t work?
faqs.a: Yes. Somatic therapy targets trauma stored in the body and nervous system, which talk therapy may miss, making it effective for those feeling stuck.

faqs.q: What are common signs of childhood emotional neglect in adults?
faqs.a: Signs include difficulty identifying feelings, chronic emptiness, low self-esteem, and trouble forming close relationships.

faqs.q: Is it necessary to work with a therapist for somatic healing?
faqs.a: While self-healing tools exist, working with a somatic therapist is recommended to safely navigate intense sensations and trauma.
